title: SEGMENT 10 — Rick Slayman: the first pig kidney transplant, March 2024

lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — Rick Slayman: the first pig kidney transplant, March 2024
Welcome back.
I hope you took your break.
The story of 2024 begins on March 16, in Boston, at Massachusetts General Hospital.
The patient is Richard Slayman — Rick to everyone who knew him — a 62-year-old man from Weymouth, Massachusetts, with end-stage kidney disease.
He had been on dialysis for seven years.
He had received a human kidney transplant in 2018, but that transplanted kidney had failed in 2023, and he had returned to dialysis.

He was, in the clinical phrase, highly sensitized — meaning his immune system had developed broad antibody reactivity from the prior transplant, making it very hard to find a compatible second human kidney.
His dialysis access was failing.
He was running out of options.
The eGenesis pig, with the full 69-edit package, had been advancing toward clinical use, and Mass General — under the leadership of transplant surgeon Dr.
Tatsuo Kawai and nephrologist Dr.
Leonardo Riella — had been preparing for this for years.
They had FDA authorization, under the same compassionate use pathway that had been used for Bennett.
They had an eGenesis donor ready.
And they had a patient.
The kidney came out of an eGenesis pig in their Massachusetts barrier facility, was transported under controlled conditions, and was implanted in Rick Slayman in a four-hour operation.
The implant site was the standard one for a kidney transplant — the right iliac fossa, in the lower abdomen.
The pig kidney was sewn into Slayman's iliac artery and iliac vein.
The ureter was sewn into the bladder.
The clamps came off.
The kidney pinked up.
It started producing urine.
He went to the ICU.
He recovered.
His creatinine — the standard blood marker of kidney function — came down from the dialysis-dependent range to nearly normal.
He was discharged from the hospital two weeks after surgery.
He went home.
He gave interviews.
He said he felt good, that he wanted to encourage other patients to consider the option.
He played with his grandchildren.
He died seven weeks after the surgery, on May 11, 2024.
The cause, according to the family and Mass General's statement, was not the pig kidney.
The kidney itself, on autopsy, was functioning, with no signs of acute rejection.
Slayman died of an unrelated cardiac event — likely a consequence of his underlying long-standing cardiovascular disease, which had been deteriorating for years before the transplant.
From the team's perspective, the kidney had worked.
The first xenotransplanted kidney from a multi-edit pig had functioned in a human patient for seven weeks without rejection.
The proof of concept for the kidney program was in.
I want to emphasize what that meant.
The Bennett heart had survived 60 days, but had been damaged by a pig virus and ultimately failed.
The Slayman kidney had survived 49 days with no signs of rejection at all, until the patient died of something else.
The kidney program, on its first try, had cleared a different bar — the immunology was holding.
The genetic engineering was doing what it was supposed to do.
The Mass General team published the case in the New England Journal of Medicine later that year.
The paper is, by the standards of clinical case reports, dramatic — but it's also restrained, with the appropriate caveats.
One patient.
Seven weeks.
We need more.
They had more, almost immediately.
